1: ʿārafel (H6205) — thick/murky darkness — is the word for the dark cloud of divine presence (Exod 20:21: ‘Moses drew near to the thick darkness where God was’; Deut 4:11; 5:22; Ps 18:9; 97:2). Solomon’s opening citation of YHWH’s word is not from a known Torah text but recalls the Sinai cloud-theology: the God who is unapproachably holy (darkness) is nevertheless the God whose glory visibly descends (fire, cloud). The verse sets up the tension the prayer will explore: a transcendent God who pledges to dwell in one house.
2: bêt zĕbûl — an exalted house; zĕbûl (H2073) means height/lofty dwelling. The word appears in the divine title baal zĕbûl (lord of the high house). Solomon uses elevated royal vocabulary to describe the temple, then immediately (v18) asks whether any building can contain the God of heaven. The tension is deliberate: the house is maximally exalted (zĕbûl) yet infinitely inadequate. lĕšibtᵉḵā lĕʿôlāmîm (a place for you to dwell in forever) — lĕʿôlāmîm is an intensified plural of eternity; Solomon prays that this house be a permanent divine address.
3: Solomon pānāh (turned, H6437) from facing the altar to face the assembly — a significant liturgical pivot. The verb marks a change of audience: Solomon has been directing his hands toward the altar (v12); now he turns to bless the people. yᵉḇāreḵ — Piel of bāraḵ (bless) — the Piel is the intensive/causative blessing form, the word used for the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26). Solomon assumes a quasi-priestly role as the king who blesses the covenant assembly.
4: bāraḵ + millēʾ (has fulfilled) — the primary theological claim of the speech: YHWH’s word (peh, mouth) has been matched by his deeds (yādô, hand). The mouth-hand correspondence is a signature of divine faithfulness: YHWH does not merely speak; what his mouth declares his hand performs (Num 23:19: ‘God is not a man, that he should lie... Has he spoken, and will he not do it?’). The perfect tense of millēʾ (has fulfilled) marks the promise as now complete.
5: The Chronicler emphasizes that YHWH chose lōʾ-bāḥartî (I did not choose) any city or any man until David — the negative formulation before the positive choice heightens the sovereignty of election. bāḥar (H977, to choose/elect) is the election vocabulary used throughout Deuteronomy for YHWH’s choice of Israel (Deut 7:6) and later of Jerusalem (Deut 12:5). The choice of Jerusalem and David are presented as simultaneous acts of divine will.
6: lišmî (for my name) — the Deuteronomic Name theology is explicit: Jerusalem was chosen for YHWH’s name (šēm) to dwell there, not YHWH himself in spatial totality. The distinction is crucial: šēm theology means God’s authority, revelation, and representational presence are located at the temple while his transcendent being remains everywhere. This prevents crude localization while affirming genuine divine engagement at a specific earthly place. David was chosen as lĭhyôt nāgîḏ (to be ruler/prince) — nāgîḏ rather than melek (king) subtly emphasizes David’s servant-leadership role.
7: hayāh ʿim lĕḇāḇ (it was with the heart of) — the idiom means ‘it was David’s intention/desire.’ The Chronicler is intensely interested in the lēḇāḇ (heart) as the seat of intention. David’s desire to build the temple was in his lēḇāḇ even if not realized — YHWH honored the intention. This will become a major Chronicler theme: the heart’s disposition toward God matters as much as external acts (1 Chr 28:9; 29:17; 2 Chr 25:2: ‘but not with a whole heart’).
8: hĕṭîḇôtā — Hiphil perfect of yāṭaḇ (to do well/be good) — ‘you did well.’ YHWH commends David’s intention: the desire to build for YHWH’s name was praiseworthy even though David was not the appointed builder. This distinguishes motivation (commendable) from execution (divinely redirected). The pattern — good intention, divine redirection, honoring the intention — appears repeatedly: Moses desired to enter Canaan (denied, yet honored); Josiah wanted to restore worship (accepted, though partial).
9: bĕnᵉḵā haYYôṣēʾ mimMēʿêḵā (your son who comes from your own loins) — the biological specificity of the Davidic succession. mēʿeh (entrails/womb, H4578) is used of offspring from one’s body. The oracle distinguishes between David’s general descendants and the specific son hûʾ-yiḇneh lišmî (he himself will build for my name) — the emphatic pronoun hûʾ marks Solomon as the definitively appointed builder, not merely a successor.
10: waYYāqem YHWH ʾeT-dᵉḇārô (the LORD has kept/established his word) — qûm in the Hiphil means to make something stand firm, to establish, to fulfill a word given. The same root qûm appears in qûmāh (v41: arise!) addressed to YHWH. Solomon describes YHWH’s word as something that has been made to stand — faithfulness as structural firmness. ʾaqqûm taḥaT dāwîḏ (I rose up in the place of David) — the succession language uses the same root qûm, creating a wordplay: the son who rose up (qûm) fulfills the word that YHWH caused to rise up (Hiphil qûm).
11: ʾărôn bᵉrît YHWH — the ark of the covenant of YHWH. The ʾārôn (ark) is the physical symbol of the covenant, the container of the tablets (H3778 lûḥôT haḇᵉrît). Its placement in the temple’s inner sanctuary completes the Davidic temple project: the ark has come home. The Chronicler stresses that the ark contains the bᵉrît (covenant) — the temple’s legitimacy rests on housing the covenant document, making it a covenant-house rather than merely a royal religious center.
12: Solomon stood lipnê mizbaḥ YHWH (before the altar of YHWH) and stretched out his hands (waYYiprōś kaPPāyw). prāś (spread/stretch out) + kap (palm of hand) is the standard OT posture for petition prayer (Ps 28:2; 44:20; 88:9; Isa 1:15: ‘when you spread out your hands...’). The posture expresses openness and receptiveness, the opposite of the clenched fist. Solomon’s prayer posture before the altar — not behind it as a priest — positions him as intercessor between the people and YHWH.
13: The bronze kiyyôr (platform/basin) — actually kiYYôr (H3595) means a portable cooking-pot or basin, here used for a raised platform. This is unique to Chronicles; Samuel-Kings does not mention it. Solomon knelt (yiḵraʿ ʿal birKāyw, knelt on his knees) and spread his hands toward heaven — two postures combined: prostration before YHWH (knees) and petition upward (hands toward heaven). The combination of humility (down) and petition (up) captures the paradox of prayer: addressing the transcendent God from a posture of creaturely submission.
14: ʾên kāmôḵā ʾĕlōhîm (there is no God like you) — the incomparability formula used throughout the OT (Exod 15:11; Ps 86:8; Isa 40:25). It is not a denial of other gods’ existence but an affirmation of YHWH’s incomparable superiority. bĕrît wĕḥesed — the pair ‘covenant and steadfast love’ — bĕrît (H1285) is the formal covenant structure; ḥesed (H2617) is the relational quality of loyal-love within the covenant. The two together describe YHWH as both structurally faithful (covenant) and relationally warm (ḥesed). bĕkol-lēḇāḇām (with all their heart) — the Deuteronomic whole-heart devotion that conditions covenant blessing.
15: ûḇᵉyāḏᵉḵā millēʾtāh (and with your hand you have fulfilled it) — the mouth-hand correspondence repeated from v4 in personal address: what YHWH spoke (bᵉpîḵā) he fulfilled (bᵉyāḏᵉḵā). The day (haYYôm hazZeh) marking the fulfillment makes the dedication moment theologically significant — Solomon is at the hinge-point between promise and fulfillment. The perfect tense of both verbs (dibbartā, you spoke; millēʾtā, you fulfilled) frames the entire dedication as a covenant-fulfillment event.
16: lōʾ-yikkāreT lᵉḵā ʾîš miLPāNAY (there will not be cut off from you a man from before me) — the Davidic succession promise in its full form. kārat (H3772, to cut off) appears in negative: YHWH promises not to cut off the Davidic line. The idiom miLPāNAY (from before me, from my presence) means ‘serving in my presence’ — the king who reigns is understood to reign in YHWH’s presence, as his representative. The conditional clause: raq ʾim-yišmᵉrû bānêḵā ʾeT-darKāM (only if your sons guard their way) — the Mosaic condition remains.
17: yēʾāmen dᵉḇāreḵā (may your word stand firm/be verified) — ʾāman (H539) in the Niphal means to be confirmed, proved reliable. The same root gives us ʾāmēn (so be it/truly). Solomon’s petition has the character of affirming what YHWH has already said: ‘may your word prove itself true.’ This is not doubt but the prayer-form of faith: holding YHWH to his own commitments. The prayer for verification of the word is the most basic form of covenant-prayer.
18: hāʾumnām yēšēḇ ʾĕlōhîm ʾeT-hāʾādām ʿal-hāʾāreṣ — ‘will God truly dwell with human beings on the earth?’ The particle hāʾumnām (H552, truly/in very deed) marks a rhetorical question that expects a negative answer — no, obviously God cannot be contained. hāʾādām uses the generic H120 (ʾādām), humanity in its earthly creatureliness. hinnēh haššāmayim ûšĕmê haššāmayim lōʾ yᵉḵalkᵉlûḵā — the double heaven (‘heavens and the heaven of heavens’) cannot contain YHWH — the spatial hyperbole establishes absolute divine transcendence before the prayer petitions for divine immanence. The tension between vv18 and 40-42 (where Solomon asks YHWH to open his eyes toward this house) is the theological heart of the prayer.
19: tᵉpillāh (H8605, prayer) + tᵉḥinnāh (H8467, supplication/plea for mercy) — the pair appears repeatedly in this chapter (vv20, 24, 25, 29, 35, 39). Tᵉḥinnāh derives from ḥānan (grace/favor) — it is a plea addressed to one in a superior position, requesting favor not owed. Solomon’s petition that YHWH attend to ‘the cry and the prayer’ (ʾel-haRinnāh wĕʾel haT tᵉpillāh) — rinnāh (H7440) means a shout, a ringing cry — combines joyful proclamation with formal petition.
20: lihyôt ʿênêḵā pĕtûḥôT (may your eyes be open) — pāqaḥ (H6491, to open eyes) is used specifically of divine attention and human enlightenment. The petition asks that YHWH’s eyes (ʿênayim) be permanently directed toward the temple. lamMāqôm ʾăšer ʾāmartā lāśûm šĕmeḵā šām — the place where YHWH said he would put his name — śûm šēm (place the name) is the standard Deuteronomic idiom for divine election of a place. The temple is defined entirely by the divine name-placement, not by its physical grandeur.
21: tiššᵉmaʿ miMMĕqôm šiḇtᵉḵā min-haššāmayim — hear from the place of your sitting-enthroned from the heavens. Šēḇet (H3427, dwell/sit/be enthroned) describes YHWH as one who sits enthroned in heaven, not locally present in the temple — the temple is where prayer is directed, not where God physically resides. wĕsālaḥTā (and forgive) — the Qal perfect used as a jussive or expected consequence. The three-part structure of Solomon’s petition-answers: šāmaʿ (hear from heaven) → sālaḥ (forgive) → result (restoration or justice). This structure repeats in all seven cases.
22: The seven petition cases begin. Case 1: ʾāšar neʾĕšam lᵉrēʿēhû (when someone sins against their neighbor). ʾāšam (H816, to be guilty/incur guilt) is the guilt-offering root — the action brings objective guilt, not just subjective wrongdoing. wĕnāśāʾ ʾālāyw ʾālāh (and is required to take an oath) — ʾālāh (H423) is a sworn oath with built-in curse if violated. The case addresses judicial disputes resolved by oath-swearing at the altar — a recognized legal mechanism (Exod 22:11).
23: wᵉšāphaṭTā ʾeT-ʿăḇāḏêḵā — and you will judge your servants. YHWH is petitioned not merely to observe but to actively judge: hārāšāʿ (to condemn the guilty, Hiphil of rāšaʿ) + hāṣaDdîq (to vindicate the righteous, Hiphil of ṣādaQ). Both are legal declarations — the Hiphil of ṣādaQ is the formal judicial act of declaring righteous/not guilty (cf. Deut 25:1; Prov 17:15). Solomon asks YHWH to be the ultimate court of appeal whose verdicts are genuinely just.
24: Case 2: military defeat — wᵉhinnāgēf ʿamᵉḵā yiśrāʾēl lipnê ʾôyēḇ (when your people Israel are defeated before an enemy). nāgaP (H5062, to strike/defeat) in the Niphal — to be struck, suffer military defeat. The cause: kî yeḥeṭᵉʾû lāḵ (because they sinned against you) — the Deuteronomic theology of defeat as covenant consequence (Deut 28:25). The restoration: wᵉšāḇû (they turn back) + wĕhôdû ʾeT-šĕmeḵā (and confess/acknowledge your name) — confession uses yāḏāh (H3034, Hiphil) which means both to confess/admit and to give thanks.
25: wĕhēḇēʾTā ʾôtām ʾel-hāʾādāmāh — and bring them back to the land. The restoration of exiled people to their land is the promised outcome of faithful repentance — the Deuteronomic return pattern (Deut 30:3-5: ‘YHWH your God will restore your fortunes and have compassion on you, gathering you again from all the peoples’). This petition (case 2) anticipates the Babylonian exile and return by centuries — Solomon’s prayer is calibrated to a history not yet fully written.
26: Case 3: drought — bĕhiʿāṣēr haššāmayim wĕlōʾ-yihyeh māṭār (when the sky is shut and there is no rain). ʿāṣar (H6113, to restrain/shut) in the Niphal — the sky being shut/held back is the reverse of the divine blessing of opened heavens (Gen 7:11; Deut 28:12). māṭār (rain) is a covenant gift — withheld as covenant discipline (Deut 28:24: ‘YHWH will make the rain of your land dust’). The petitioner’s response: wĕhitPaLLĕlû ʾel hamMāqôm hazZeh (they pray toward this place) — the temple becomes the focal point of petition even when the sky itself seems closed.
27: wĕhôrêTām ʾeT-hadDereḵ haṭṭôḇāh (teach them the good/right way to walk) — instruction (yārāh, H3384, the root of tôrāh) as part of the divine response to repentance. YHWH’s response to returning people is not merely to restore conditions but to provide instruction for the future. wĕnātattāh māṭār ʿal-ʾarṣeḵā (give rain on your land) — the rain that was withheld as discipline is now given as grace. The same land that suffered drought under judgment receives rain under restoration.
28: Case 4 opens a catalogue of disasters: rāʿāḇ kî-yihyeh bāʾāreṣ (famine), deber kî-yihyeh (plague/pestilence), šiddāpôn ûyērāqôn (blight and mildew/blasting and blighting — agricultural diseases), ʾarBeh wĕḥāsîl (locust and swarming locust/grasshopper). The list echoes the covenant curses of Deut 28:21-22,38-40: these are the consequences of covenant breaking, now brought under the scope of the temple’s intercessory function.
29: kol-tᵉpillāh kol-tᵉḥinnāh (every prayer, every supplication) — the universal scope: kol (all/every) before both prayer words extends the petition to every individual case, not merely the corporate situations previously described. ʾăšer yihyeh lᵉḵol-hāʾādām (whatever belongs to every person) — the switch to hāʾādām (human beings) rather than ʿamᵉḵā (your people) suggests inclusion of any human in distress. maKKāTô ûmaḵʾôḇô (their pain and their grief) — maKKāh (wound/blow) + maḵʾôḇ (pain/sorrow): each person’s unique suffering is known to YHWH, not merely the corporate condition.
30: kî ʾattāh lᵉḇadᵉḵā yāḏaʿtā (for you alone know) — the absolute divine omniscience formula. lᵉḇadᵉḵā (you alone, you exclusively) marks YHWH’s heart-knowledge as uniquely divine: only he knows every heart. yāḏaʿtā (you have known, perfect tense) — the completed-action perfect for an eternal truth: YHWH has always known and continues to know. ʾeT-lĕḇaḇ bᵉnê hāʾādām — the hearts of the sons of humanity — lēḇāḇ (H3824, inner person/heart) is the seat of will, thought, and intention. What humans see externally, YHWH sees at the level of the heart.
31: lᵉyirʾāh ʾôtᵉḵā (so that they may fear you) — yārēʾ (H3372, to fear/revere) is the practical outcome Solomon prays for. The purpose of YHWH’s responsive justice is not punishment per se but the formation of the fear of God — the reverential recognition that YHWH sees and judges. lāleḵeT bidᵉrāḵêḵā (to walk in your ways) — covenant obedience expressed as halāḵāh-language (walking in ways): Hebrew moral life is directional movement, not merely rule-following. The fear of YHWH producing covenant walk is the Deuteronomic anthropological ideal.
32: Case 5: wĕgam ʾel-haNoḵrî ʾăšer lōʾ-mēʿamᵉḵā yiśrāʾēl hûʾ (and also for the foreigner who is not from your people Israel). noḵrî (H5236, foreigner/alien) — specifically a non-Israelite, distinct from gēr (resident alien living within Israel). Solomon petitions for the foreigner who comes mēʾeres rĕḥôqāh (from a distant land) because of YHWH’s great name (lĕšimᵉḵā haGGādôl) and mighty arm (yādᵉḵā haḥăzāqāh + zᵉrôʿăḵā haNNᵉṭûyāh). The Exodus language (‘mighty hand and outstretched arm’) applied to the Gentile’s motive for prayer is remarkable — Gentiles are drawn to YHWH by the reputation of his saving acts for Israel.
33: wĕʿāśîtā kol ʾăšer yiqrā ʾēlêḵā haNoḵrî — grant everything the foreigner calls upon you for. qārāʾ (call upon, appeal to) is the standard prayer verb. The petition’s goal: lĕmaʿan yēdĕʿû kol-ʿammê hāʾāreṣ ʾeT-šĕmeḵā (so that all peoples of the earth may know your name). The universal dimension of Solomon’s prayer is explicit: the temple is a place of prayer not for Israel alone but for all peoples (kol-ʿammê hāʾāreṣ). yirʾûḵā (to fear you) — the same goal as v31 — the fear of YHWH extending to all peoples through answered prayer at the temple.
34: Case 6: warfare — kî-yēṣēʾ ʿamᵉḵā lammilḥāmāh (when your people go out to war). The prayer directs toward hāʿîr hazZōʾT (this city) + habBayiT (this house) — the dual anchor point. hadDereḵ ʾăšer tišlāḥēm (wherever you send them) — YHWH as the one who sends the army; the troops go only where YHWH commissions. The petition appeals to YHWH as the divine warrior who supports those he sends.
35: wĕšāmaʿtā min-haššāmayim ʾeT-tᵉpillātām wĕʾeT-tᵉḥinnātām — the standard hearing-petition formula. wĕʿāśîtā mišpāṭām (uphold their cause) — mišpāṭ (H4941) here means ‘cause/right/legal case’ rather than merely ‘judgment.’ YHWH is petitioned to act as the divine advocate who prosecutes on behalf of those he has sent. The pairing of šāmaʿ (hear) and ʿāśāh mišpāṭ (do justice/uphold cause) frames YHWH as judge-intercessor.
36: kî ʾên ʾādām ʾăšer lōʾ yeḥeṭāʾ — for there is no human being who does not sin. This is one of the OT’s clearest universal sinfulness statements. ʾādām (humanity) + lōʾ yeḥeṭāʾ (does not sin, imperfect of ḥāṭāʾ): the imperfect suggests habitual or potential action — no human consistently avoids sin. Case 7 (exile) is introduced: wĕʾānaptā bām (you are angry with them) — ʾānap (H0639/599, to be angry) — the divine anger as the theological cause of exile.
37: wĕhiShîḇû ʾel-libbām (and they return it to their heart/they take it to heart) — šûḇ with lēḇāḇ describes an interior turning, a change of mind and will, not merely outward behavior change. ʾāšanû wĕhirShânû wĕrāšāʿnû — we have sinned, we have done wrong, we have acted wickedly — three escalating confession verbs. ʾāšam (incurred guilt), hēraʿ (done evil), rāšaʿ (acted wickedly) — the triple confession formula covers the full range of moral failure: objective guilt, practical wrongdoing, wicked character.
38: wᵉšāḇû ʾēlêḵā bᵉkol-libbām ûḇᵉḵol-napšām — they return to you with all their heart and with all their soul. The kol-lēḇāḇ + kol-nepeš formula (whole heart + whole soul) is the Deuteronomic love-commandment vocabulary (Deut 6:5: ‘love YHWH your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength’). Repentance is nothing less than the restoration of the first-commandment wholeness. darKām (their way/direction of life) + ʿîrᵉḵā (your city) + habBayiT (this house) — the three-point geographical prayer anchor: land, city, temple.
39: wĕšāmaʿtā min-haššāmayim mimmĕqôm šiḇtᵉḵā — hear from heaven, from the place of your sitting/enthronement. The spatial logic is now fully established: YHWH is enthroned in heaven (šēḇet, dwelling/enthroned); prayer rises from earth toward the temple; YHWH’s response descends from heaven. The temple is the intersection-point, not YHWH’s residence. wĕsālaḥtā lĕʿamᵉḵā — forgive your people: sālaḥ (H5545, to forgive/pardon) — this verb is used exclusively of YHWH in the OT; humans can pardon (nāśāʾ, lift/bear/forgive) but only YHWH sālaḥ. The divine prerogative of forgiveness is linguistically enforced.
40: ʿattāh ʾĕlōhay yihyû-nāʾ ʿênêḵā pĕtûḥôT — now, my God, may your eyes be open. The concluding petition mirrors v20’s opening request: the prayer is framed by this open-eyes image. wĕʾoznêḵā qašûḇôT (and your ears attentive) — qāšaḇ (H7181, to pay attention/listen carefully) in the Qal passive: ears that are set to attentive mode. The pairing of open eyes and attentive ears represents full divine awareness: nothing concerning this house escapes YHWH’s perception.
41: wᵉʿattāh qûmāh YHWH ʾĕlōhîm lᵉnûḥāteḵā — the invocation drawn from Ps 132:8 verbatim: ‘arise, LORD God, to your resting place.’ qûm (arise, H6965) — the command to YHWH to move, to act. mᵉnûḥāh (H4496, resting place, rest) — the word describes the condition of settled, untroubled security. The ark of YHWH’s power (ʾărôn ʿuzzeḵā) accompanies YHWH to his rest: the liturgical installation of the ark completes the journey that began when David brought it to Jerusalem. kôhănêḵā... yilbᵉšû yᵉšûʿāh (may your priests be clothed with salvation) — lāḇaš + yᵉšûʿāh is clothing-with-victory: priestly vestments as embodied declaration of divine saving power.
42: ʾal-tāšēḇ pᵉnê mᵉšîḥeḵā — do not turn away the face of your anointed. šûḇ pānîm (turn away the face) means to reject a petitioner — the royal gesture of refusal (1 Kgs 2:16-17: ‘do not turn away my face’). mᵉšîḥeḵā (H4899, your anointed one) — from māšaḥ (to anoint), the technical term for the royal or priestly anointing that designates YHWH’s chosen agent. Solomon applies the term to himself; the Psalms apply it to the ideal Davidic king (Ps 2:2; 18:50; 20:6; 89:38,51). The prayer closes with the messianic title — the anointed one’s standing before YHWH depends on the covenant faithfulness (ḥasdê dāwîḏ, your steadfast love for David) rather than personal merit.
